title: "The deviance bias of congenial multiple imputation, with applications to likelihood-ratio comparison and model selection"
subtitle: "JAIGP submission — working draft"
author:
  - name: "Marcus Waldman"
    note: "ORCID-verified human prompter of an AI-assisted derivation; provenance per JAIGP norms (see the statement at the end)."
date: last-modified

  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    embed-resources: true

::: {.callout-note}
**Draft status.** This is the submission document in progress. The canonical *sourced
derivation* — every external claim pinned to a source passage, every number pinned to a
committed artifact, executable verification inline — is the companion document
`manuscript/derivation.qmd`; this paper states results and points there for the full
provenance apparatus.
:::

# Introduction {#sec-intro}

Multiple imputation answers a practical question with an elegant division of labor: an
imputer fills in the missing values several times from a model for the complete data, an
analyst fits whatever model they care about to each completed data set, and simple rules
combine the results [@rubinMultipleImputationNonresponse1987]. For point estimates and
variances the combining rules are settled. For *likelihood-based* quantities they are not.
The averaged log-likelihood across imputations — the Q-function of the EM algorithm in its
infinite-imputation limit, and the computational backbone of the modern MI
likelihood-ratio test of @chanMultipleImprovementsMultiple2022, which repairs the
combining rule of @mengPerformingLikelihoodRatio1992b by maximizing the average rather
than averaging the maxima — is *not* the observed-data log-likelihood, and it
is not an unbiased stand-in for the complete-data log-likelihood either. Deviances built
from it are systematically too favorable.

This paper derives exactly how favorable. For a regular likelihood model fit on
congenially imputed data, with an estimated scale or covariance, the averaged
log-likelihood at the observed-data maximum likelihood estimate overstates its
complete-data target by
$$ \tfrac{1}{2}\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}) \;+\; (A){+}(C), $$
where $\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ is the trace of the relative-increase-in-variance
matrix — the total odds of missing information about the model's parameters — and
$(A)+(C)$ is an $O(1)$ design-imbalance term that vanishes under MCAR and is nonzero under
MAR exactly when the missing and observed units differ on the conditioning variables. The
half-trace itself decomposes into two parts with opposite signs and distinct mechanisms:
an *imputation-bias* term $+\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ (completions drawn from the
imputation model inflate the apparent fit of that same model) and an
*estimation-mismatch* term
$-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ (the MI fit targets the complete-data
maximizer, which is not the observed-data one). The decomposition, the MAR term, and the
scope condition — a known-scale, location-only fit loses the headline bias entirely,
collapsing to zero under deterministic imputation and to
$-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ under proper MI — are stated as the
main theorem in @sec-setup and derived in full in the companion document.

Two applications follow.

**Likelihood-ratio comparison.** When two nested models are fit to the *same* imputations,
almost everything cancels — but not everything. At the null the deviance numerator carries
a differential bias equal to $\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}_\perp)$, the missing
information of the tested directions projected in the *complete-data* metric — provably
*not* the difference of the two models' own trace corrections, which always overstates it.
The same sharing of imputations collapses the per-data-set noise of the comparison from
$O(\sqrt n)$ to $O(1)$, and cancels the design-imbalance term exactly at the null. At the
null, this differential bias is precisely the mean that a correctly calibrated reference
distribution already absorbs [@chanGeneralFeasibleTests2022] — so tests built on such
references are safe, and the bias affects exactly the procedures that refer the statistic
to no reference distribution at all: information-criterion ranking, explicit numerator
corrections, and non-nested comparison (@sec-lrt).

**Model selection.** Information criteria are exactly such a procedure: MI-AIC compares
penalized averaged deviances as numbers. The bias is model-specific — each candidate's
criterion is too small by its own $\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ — so uncorrected
MI-AIC systematically favors the candidates with the *most* missing information about
their own parameters. In the congenial cells of our pre-registered study, every
misclassification of the uncorrected criterion lands on the largest-RIV candidate. Adding
$\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}_k)$ per candidate repairs the mean; as a penalty, the
repaired criterion reproduces the missing-data information criterion
$\mathrm{AIC}_{x;y}$ of @shimodairaInformationCriterionModel2017, whose missing-data
surcharge is half that of the earlier complete-data-discrepancy criteria
[@cavanaughAkaikeInformationCriterion1998a]. The mean is not the whole story: we derive,
pre-register, and then measure a three-step contrast — matching the mean, the first two
moments, or the entire per-model null distribution of the criterion — and find that the
achievable target splits along the null/noncentral axis: the null side calibrates
completely (the distribution-matched criterion selects indistinguishably from
complete-data AIC in the pre-registered design), while no transform built from the
observed data can restore the evidence the missing data destroyed, and the two stronger
corrections shrink noncentral statistics by an exactly predictable factor (@sec-ic).

**Relation to existing work.** The result sits upstream of the MI test-calibration
literature. Meng and Rubin's combining rule for likelihood-ratio tests
[@mengPerformingLikelihoodRatio1992b], its repair by Chan and Meng
[@chanMultipleImprovementsMultiple2022], and Chan's general stacked-imputation tests
[@chanGeneralFeasibleTests2022] all concern how to *calibrate the reference distribution*
of an MI test statistic, taking the statistic's numerator as given. We address the *bias
of that numerator*. The two are complementary: calibration makes the reference
distribution right; the present correction makes the statistic referred to it centered.
On the model-selection side, three routes to a complete-data-equivalent criterion
predate this work. The complete-data-discrepancy criteria PDIO and AICcd
[@cavanaughAkaikeInformationCriterion1998a] attach a $2\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$
missing-data penalty under EM estimation; @shimodairaInformationCriterionModel2017 showed
that a weaker assumption halves it, giving $\mathrm{AIC}_{x;y}$ with penalty
$\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$. The missing-covariate criteria of
@claeskensconsentinoVariableSelectionIncomplete2008 target a different
(EM-adjusted-likelihood) discrepancy with a Takeuchi-type penalty, and so sit outside
this line. Hens, Aerts and Molenberghs [@hensModelSelectionIncomplete2006a] reach the
complete-data target by inverse-probability reweighting of the complete cases: their
route needs estimated selection probabilities, keeps the penalty at $2q$, and explicitly
leaves an *imputation*-based criterion open — the criterion this paper supplies. The
design-imbalance bias their analysis diagnoses (the complete-case discrepancy minimizer
shifts by $\operatorname{Cov}(y,\pi)/\mathbb{E}[\pi]$ under MAR) is the same phenomenon
our $(A)+(C)$ term captures intrinsically within MI.

**What is new, and what is not.** The trace *penalty* for model selection is not new — it
is Shimodaira's $\mathrm{AIC}_{x;y}$, and the doubled version goes back to
@cavanaughAkaikeInformationCriterion1998a and Shimodaira's own PDIO. The calibration of MI
*test* references is not new either — that is the Meng–Rubin line culminating in
@chanGeneralFeasibleTests2022. What is new here: (i) the *decomposition* of the deviance
bias into imputation bias and estimation mismatch, with the estimated-scale scope
condition; (ii) the MAR design-imbalance term $(A)+(C)$, which lies beyond the
fixed-pattern i.i.d. setting of @shimodairaInformationCriterionModel2017 — its concluding
section names the combination of a missing mechanism with other sampling mechanisms, such
as covariate shift [@shimodairahidetoshiImprovingPredictiveInference2000], as future
work; and (iii) the extension from the deterministic EM Q-function to proper multiple
imputation. Two application-level results are also, to our knowledge, new: the exact
differential bias of the MI likelihood-ratio numerator — the complete-data-metric
projection, the proof that the naive difference of trace corrections always overstates
it, the $O(1)$ paired-noise collapse, and the exact null cancellation of the
design-imbalance differential — and the pre-registered distribution-matching contrast
for model selection with its null/noncentral split. Uncongenial settings
[@mengMultipleImputationInferencesUncongenial1994a] are out of scope throughout:
congeniality is assumed, and the bias derived here is what remains *after* the imputer
and analyst agree.

@sec-background restates the standard results this argument is built from, each in its
source's own terms. @sec-setup fixes notation and states the main theorem.
@sec-lrt and @sec-ic develop the two applications. @sec-numerics reports the
pre-registered numerical studies. @sec-discussion states what is firm and what is not.
Throughout, the companion sourced derivation (`manuscript/derivation.qmd`) carries the
full proofs, the source-passage audit trail, and the executable symbolic verification;
this paper is self-contained at the level of statements and evidence.

# Background {#sec-background}

Every step of the argument rests on results that are individually standard. We restate
them here rather than merely cite them, because each is used later with a precision that
a casual recollection would miss — the EM rate matrix is *not* the RIV, Rubin's rules are
*exact* given one named approximation, the observed and expected information *differ*
under MAR — and each restatement names the proposition that depends on it.

**Ignorability.** @rubinInferenceMissingData1976b gives the weakest general conditions
under which the process that causes missing data can be ignored: for direct-likelihood
and Bayesian inference it suffices that the missing data are *missing at random* (the
conditional probability of the observed missingness pattern is the same for all values of
the missing data) and that the mechanism's parameter is *distinct* from the data
parameter. Sampling-distribution inference is stricter: it requires in addition that the
observed data are *observed at random* — jointly amounting to MCAR — and is otherwise
generally conditional on the observed pattern. Everything below assumes the
likelihood-side conditions (ignorable missingness); the gap Rubin identified between the
likelihood and sampling-distribution senses of "ignorable" is precisely where two later
objects live: the MAR design-imbalance term of the main theorem, and the
information-matrix distinction of @kenwardLikelihoodBasedFrequentist1998 restated at the
end of this section.

**EM and the Q-function.** @dempsterMaximumLikelihoodIncomplete1977a define, at their
final level of generality,
$$ Q(\phi' \mid \phi) \;=\; E\big(\log f(\mathbf{x} \mid \phi') \,\big|\, \mathbf{y}, \phi\big), $$ {#eq-bg-q}
the expected complete-data log-likelihood given the observed data, with the E-step
computing $Q(\cdot \mid \phi^{(p)})$ and the M-step maximizing it; their heuristic is
that "since we do not know $\log f(\mathbf{x}\mid\phi)$, we maximize instead its current
expectation given the data $\mathbf{y}$ and the current fit $\phi^{(p)}$." Two facts from
their Section 3 are load-bearing later. First, the decomposition
$Q(\phi'\mid\phi) = L(\phi') + H(\phi'\mid\phi)$, where $L$ is the observed-data
log-likelihood and $H$ is a conditional expectation of the log conditional density of the
complete data — a (negative) conditional-entropy term. The bias theorem of @sec-setup is,
at bottom, an account of what the $H$ term does when its parameters are estimated rather
than known. Second, the curvature of $Q$ at the maximizer is the *complete-data*
information, and the EM rate of convergence is governed by the missing fraction: the
negative curvature of the $H$ term "is an expected or Fisher information in the
unobserved part" of the data, the rate depends on its size relative to the observed-data
information, and "if the information loss due to incompleteness is
small, then the algorithm converges rapidly." That the Q-function's curvature is
$I_{\text{com}}$ — not $I_{\text{obs}}$ — is the single geometric fact behind the
likelihood-ratio result of @sec-lrt.

**The missing-information principle and the EM rate matrix.**
@woodburyMissingInformationPrinciple1972 decompose the information in the complete data
as $J_X = J_Y + J_{X/Y}$ — the observed-data information plus what they call the lost
information — together with the score identity
$\operatorname{Sco}(\theta \mid Y) = E[\operatorname{Sco}(\theta \mid X) \mid Y]$.
@Meng01121991 state the same identity in the form used throughout this paper,
$$ I_{\text{obs}} \;=\; I_{\text{com}} - I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}, $$ {#eq-bg-mip}
"observed information = complete information − missing information, which has been called
the 'missing information principle' by Orchard and Woodbury (1972)," and build on the
EM rate matrix $DM = I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}\, I_{\text{com}}^{-1}$ — the form
established by @dempsterMaximumLikelihoodIncomplete1977a — to obtain the
increase in asymptotic variance due to missing data,
$\Delta V = I_{\text{com}}^{-1} DM (I - DM)^{-1}$. The central matrix of this paper,
$\mathrm{RIV} = I_{\text{obs}}^{-1} I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}$, is built from the same
two ingredients but normalized by $I_{\text{obs}}$, not $I_{\text{com}}$: it is *not* the
EM rate matrix, and conflating the two normalizations is a standing hazard the notation
of @sec-setup is chosen to prevent.

**Rubin's rules are exact posterior-moment identities.** For multiply-imputed data,
@rubinMultipleImputationNonresponse1987 (Result 3.2) shows that the posterior mean of an
estimand $Q$ given the observed data equals the average of the completed-data posterior
means, and the posterior variance equals the average completed-data variance plus the
variance of the completed-data means — the conditional-moment rules, applied to
imputation. With infinitely many imputations this gives, in his notation,
$\bar Q_\infty$, $\bar U_\infty$, $B_\infty$, and the total variance
$T_\infty = \bar U_\infty + B_\infty$, all of them *exact* identities, not asymptotic
approximations. Rubin then reads $T_\infty^{-1}$ as observed information and
$\bar U_\infty^{-1}$ as the expected total information if the missing data were observed,
and defines the scalar relative increase in variance due to nonresponse
$$ r_\infty \;=\; B_\infty / \bar U_\infty $$ {#eq-bg-rinf}
(his equation 3.1.7). A single approximation separates these identities from usable
inference: "the usual approximation of the posterior distribution as normal, motivated in
Section 2.10" (a Laplace-type approximation; @Tierney01031986). The matrix
$\mathrm{RIV}$ of this paper is @eq-bg-rinf in matrix form, and the theorem of @sec-setup
describes what happens when the exact moment identities are used not as moments but
through the likelihood itself.

**Imputation is Monte Carlo integration of the E-step.** @weiMonteCarloImplementation1990
implement the E-step by simulation: draws $z^{(1)},\dots,z^{(m)}$ from the conditional
predictive distribution at the current parameter value replace the integral in
@eq-bg-q by the average
$$ Q_{i+1}\big(\theta, \theta^{(i)}\big) \;=\; \frac{1}{m}\sum_{j=1}^{m} \log p\big(\theta \mid z^{(j)}, y\big), $$ {#eq-bg-mcem}
and their Remark 2 makes the bridge explicit: "Rubin (1987) referred to the quantities
$z^{(1)}, \ldots, z^{(m)}$ as multiple imputations." This is the precise sense in which
the averaged log-likelihood over imputations *is* a Q-function, and its $m \to \infty$
limit $\bar Q_\infty(\theta)$ — the central object of this paper — is the E-step integral
taken at the *fitted* imputation model. That the conditioning is on the fitted (or drawn)
parameter, not on an oracle, determines the sign of the main theorem (@sec-setup).

**Congeniality.** @mengMultipleImputationInferencesUncongenial1994a formalizes when the
imputer's model and the analyst's procedure agree: the analysis procedure is *congenial*
to the imputation model if there exists a Bayesian model whose posterior means and
variances asymptotically reproduce the analyst's complete-data and incomplete-data
procedures, and whose posterior predictive distribution for the missing data is identical
to the imputation model. Everything in this paper assumes congenial, proper imputation:
the bias we derive is not an artifact of imputer–analyst disagreement but what remains
*after* they agree.

**AIC is a bias-corrected plug-in estimate.** @akaikeNewLookStatistical1974a takes as
criterion the mean log-likelihood $S(g; f(\cdot\mid\theta)) = \int g(x)\log f(x\mid\theta)dx$
and observes that estimating it by the maximized log-likelihood "needs a correction for
the downward bias introduced by replacing $\theta$ by $\hat\theta$. This correction is
simply realized by adding $k$," giving
$$ \mathrm{AIC} \;=\; -2\log(\text{maximum likelihood}) + 2k. $$ {#eq-bg-aic}
The model-selection application of @sec-ic is this same accounting with one additional
bias source: under MI the goodness-of-fit term is $-2\bar Q_\infty$ rather than the
complete-data deviance, and the additional bias is what the main theorem quantifies.

**Wilks.** Under a null hypothesis fixing $h - m$ of $h$ parameters,
@wilksLargeSampleDistributionLikelihood1938a shows that $-2\log\lambda$ is distributed as
$\chi^2_{h-m}$ up to terms of order $n^{-1/2}$. This is the complete-data reference
against which every MI deviance in @sec-lrt and @sec-ic is ultimately compared.

**Observed versus expected information under MAR.**
@kenwardLikelihoodBasedFrequentist1998 sharpen Rubin's caution about frequentist
inference: under MAR the missingness indicator is not ancillary, the correct sampling
framework is unconditional over data *and* missingness pattern, and the "naive" expected
information — computed as if the realized pattern were fixed by design — is biased, with
MCAR necessary and sufficient for the naive and unconditional forms to agree. Their
recommendation is to use the observed information. Their Section 3.2 bivariate Gaussian
example shows where the difference lodges: under MAR dropout the unconditional
information acquires nonzero mean–covariance *cross terms* that the naive form misses.
Both facts recur in @sec-setup: the design-imbalance term $(A)+(C)$ is nonzero exactly
when MCAR fails, is computed against the realized (observed) information as
@kenwardLikelihoodBasedFrequentist1998 recommend, and the observed-data information
behind the one $\mathrm{RIV}$ of this paper carries exactly their MAR cross term.

**MI test combining and calibration.** @mengPerformingLikelihoodRatio1992b combine
complete-data likelihood-ratio statistics across imputations into a test statistic
that needs only the averaged LRT evaluated at averaged estimates, calibrated against an
$F$ reference under equal fractions of missing information.
@chanMultipleImprovementsMultiple2022 repair this procedure's defects (possible
negativity, parametrization dependence, an inconsistent missing-information estimator
under alternatives) by switching the order of averaging and maximization: their statistic
$$ \hat d_{\mathrm{L}} \;=\; 2\big\{\bar L(\hat\psi^{*}) - \bar L(\hat\psi_0^{*})\big\}, \qquad \hat\psi^{*} = \arg\max_\psi \bar L(\psi), $$ {#eq-bg-dl}
maximizes the *averaged* log-likelihood $\bar L(\psi) = m^{-1}\sum_\ell \log f(X^{(\ell)}\mid\psi)$,
which is nonnegative and invariant by construction. @chanGeneralFeasibleTests2022 then
drops the equal-fractions assumption entirely: stacking the imputed data sets yields
estimators of every eigenvalue $r_j$ of the odds-of-missing-information matrix, and the
$m \to \infty$ null law of the combined statistic is built on the weighted sum
$\sum_{j=1}^{k} (1 + r_j)\, G_j$ with independent
$G_j \sim \chi^2_1$ (his equation 1.10 normalizes this sum by $k(1 + \mu_r)$) — a sum
whose *mean* exceeds $k$ by the total odds of missing information in the tested
directions. The
numerator analyzed in @sec-lrt is exactly the maximize-then-average statistic
@eq-bg-dl, and the absorption property of Chan's reference law is what confines the
likelihood-ratio bias to procedures that use no such reference.

# Setup and main theorem {#sec-setup}

## Objects

Let $Y \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times p}$ collect $N$ independent rows
$Y_i \sim \mathcal{N}_p(\mu, \Sigma)$, $\Sigma \succ 0$, with estimand
$\theta = (\mu, \operatorname{vech}\Sigma)$ of dimension $k = p + \tfrac12 p(p+1)$. A
response indicator splits each row into observed and missing parts,
$Y = (Y_{\text{obs}}, Y_{\text{mis}})$; missingness is ignorable throughout (MAR with
distinct mechanism parameters; @sec-background). The theorem is stated for a general
regular likelihood with an estimated scale or covariance and instantiated in this
multivariate normal family; the scope of that instantiation is taken up in
@sec-discussion.

Missingness forces the distinction the whole argument turns on. The *complete-data*
log-likelihood $\ell_{\text{com}}(\theta; Y)$ is what we would have maximized had nothing
been missing, with maximizer $\hat\theta_{\text{com}}$ — the gold standard MI aims to
recover. The *observed-data* log-likelihood
$\ell_{\text{obs}}(\theta; Y_{\text{obs}})$ sums each row's marginal density over its
observed coordinates [@schaferAnalysisIncompleteMultivariate1997, §2.3], with maximizer
$\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$. Their Fisher informations $I_{\text{com}}$ and
$I_{\text{obs}}$ — expectations of the respective negative Hessians — differ by the
missing information, $I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}} := I_{\text{com}} - I_{\text{obs}}
\succeq 0$, which is @eq-bg-mip rearranged. The matrix in which the whole result is
written is the relative increase in variance,
$$ \mathrm{RIV} \;:=\; I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}\, I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}} \;=\; I_{\text{obs}}^{-1} I_{\text{com}} - I_k, \qquad \operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}) = \operatorname{tr}(I_{\text{obs}}^{-1} I_{\text{com}}) - k. $$ {#eq-riv}
Under congenial proper MI with $m \to \infty$, Rubin's combining rules give
$\bar U_\infty = I_{\text{com}}^{-1}$ and $T_\infty = I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}$, so
$\mathrm{RIV} = B_\infty \bar U_\infty^{-1}$ is exactly the matrix form of Rubin's
$r_\infty$ (@eq-bg-rinf). It must not be confused with the fraction-of-missing-information
matrix $D = I_{\text{com}}^{-1} I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}$
[@schaferAnalysisIncompleteMultivariate1997, eq. 3.21] — the transpose-similar form of
the EM rate matrix of @sec-background, with the same eigenvalues and trace — which
normalizes by $I_{\text{com}}^{-1}$ where the RIV normalizes by $I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}$;
the two relate by $D = (I_k + \mathrm{RIV})^{-1}\mathrm{RIV}$, the matrix version of
$\gamma = r/(1+r)$, and substituting one for the other changes every constant below.

One further specification pins the RIV down completely. Under MAR the response pattern is
not ancillary, so the expectation defining $I_{\text{obs}}$ must be taken
*unconditionally* over data and pattern jointly
[@kenwardLikelihoodBasedFrequentist1998]; the resulting information carries the
mean–covariance cross term of their Section 3.2 and is consistently estimated by the
observed information, with no model for the mechanism. Computed this way there is a
*single* RIV: both bias terms of the theorem calibrate to it, with no residual gap. (An
earlier reading of our own Monte Carlo output, in which the two terms appeared to attach
to different RIVs, was an artifact of grading against the naive block-diagonal
information — the MCAR form, which omits the cross term.)

The object the theorem is about is the infinite-imputation Q-function. Under congenial
proper MI the imputation parameter $\tilde\phi$ is a draw from the imputation posterior
fit to $Y_{\text{obs}}$, centered at $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$ (under deterministic FIML
imputation it collapses to the point value $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$), each completion is
drawn from $P(\cdot \mid Y_{\text{obs}}, \tilde\phi)$, and
$$ \bar Q_\infty(\theta) \;:=\; \lim_{M\to\infty} \frac1M \sum_{l=1}^{M} \ell_{\text{com}}\big(\theta;\, Y_{\text{obs}}, \tilde Y_{\text{mis}}^{(l)}\big) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\tilde\phi}\, \mathbb{E}_{Y_{\text{mis}} \mid Y_{\text{obs}}, \tilde\phi}\big[\ell_{\text{com}}(\theta)\big], $$ {#eq-qbar}
the proper-MI limit of @eq-bg-mcem. One feature of @eq-qbar determines the sign of
everything that follows: the inner expectation conditions on the *fitted* (or drawn)
parameter, exactly as the EM E-step conditions on the current iterate — not on the truth
$\theta_0$. Were $\bar Q_\infty$ instead the oracle conditional expectation
$\mathbb{E}[\ell_{\text{com}}(\theta) \mid Y_{\text{obs}}]$ under the data-generating law,
the tower property would force the imputation-bias term below to zero (because
$\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$ is a function of $Y_{\text{obs}}$ alone) and the total bias
would be $-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$, with the opposite sign. The analyst
imputes from a model fit to the data, not from an oracle, and the difference between the
two is precisely the imputation bias. Both regimes are confirmed by direct simulation:
imputing from the truth drives the imputation bias to zero; imputing from the fitted
model — deterministic FIML and proper MI alike — produces the constants of the theorem
(@sec-numerics).

The bias is split at the pivot $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$ into two terms with distinct
mechanisms,
$$ T \;=\; \underbrace{\big[\bar Q_\infty(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}) - \ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})\big]}_{A:\ \text{imputation bias}} \;+\; \underbrace{\big[\ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}) - \ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{com}})\big]}_{B:\ \text{estimation mismatch}}, $$ {#eq-split}
on the log-likelihood scale; deviance-scale statements double everything.

## Assumptions

Seven conditions are in force, none exotic. R1–R2: the complete-data model is
differentiable in quadratic mean with Lipschitz log-density and consistent MLE, and both
informations are positive definite [@vaartAsymptoticStatistics1998a, Theorem 5.39]. R3:
ignorability [@rubinInferenceMissingData1976b]. R4–R6: congeniality, second-moment
properness, and self-efficiency of the analyst's estimator
[@mengMultipleImputationInferencesUncongenial1994a;
@rubinMultipleImputationNonresponse1987]. R7: the infinite-imputation idealization;
finite-$m$ corrections are Rubin's. Together R4–R7 deliver the variance-recovery property
$T_\infty = \operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}) = I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}$ that
anchors the identification of @eq-riv with $r_\infty$.

## The theorem

::: {#thm-bias}
## Q-function deviance bias

Under R1–R7, for a model that estimates a scale or covariance — so that the conditional
missing-data entropy depends on the estimated parameters — to leading order
$$ \mathbb{E}[T] \;=\; \mathbb{E}\big[\bar Q_\infty(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}) - \ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{com}})\big] \;=\; +\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}) \;+\; \big[(A)+(C)\big] \;+\; O(n^{-1}), $$ {#eq-thm}
decomposing as $\mathbb{E}[A] = +\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}) + [(A)+(C)] + O(n^{-1})$
and $\mathbb{E}[B] = -\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}) + O(n^{-1})$. The
design-imbalance term $(A)+(C)$ vanishes under MCAR and is a nonzero $O(1)$ under MAR.
:::

*Term B* is an elementary Taylor argument. Expanding $\ell_{\text{com}}$ about its own
maximizer kills the first-order term, and
$\mathbb{E}[B] = -\tfrac12 \operatorname{tr}\big(I_{\text{com}}
\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}} - \hat\theta_{\text{com}})\big) + O(n^{-3/2})$.
The variance of the estimator *gap* is
$$ \operatorname{Var}\big(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}} - \hat\theta_{\text{com}}\big) \;=\; I_{\text{obs}}^{-1} - I_{\text{com}}^{-1}, $$ {#eq-vargap}
which follows from the asymptotic linearity of both MLEs and the score identity
$U_{\text{obs}} = \mathbb{E}[U_{\text{com}} \mid Y_{\text{obs}}]$ (the conditional-score
form of the missing-information principle; @sec-background), whence
$\operatorname{Cov}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}, \hat\theta_{\text{com}}) = I_{\text{com}}^{-1}$.
Substituting @eq-vargap and reducing the trace gives
$-\tfrac12(\operatorname{tr}(I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}I_{\text{com}}) - k) =
-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$. The one trap is substituting
$\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})$ for the variance of the gap, which would
produce a spurious $-\tfrac12 k$; @eq-vargap is stated to forestall it.

*Term A* is the delicate half. Factoring
$\ell_{\text{com}} = \ell_{\text{obs}} + \log P(Y_{\text{mis}} \mid Y_{\text{obs}}, \theta)$
cancels the $\ell_{\text{obs}}$ parts in $A$, leaving the discrepancy between averaging
the missing-data log-density under the fitted $\tilde\phi$ versus under the truth. A
second-order expansion in $\tilde\phi$ about $\theta_0$ yields three pieces: a main
cross-term, which pairs $\tilde\phi - \theta_0$ with
$\hat\theta_{\text{obs}} - \theta_0$ through $I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}$ and
contributes the leading $+\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$; an entropy-gradient piece
$(A)$, pairing the bias of $\tilde\phi$ with
$\alpha = -\mathcal{E}'(\theta_0)$, the gradient of the conditional missing-data entropy;
and a cross-entropy-curvature piece $(C)$, pairing
$\operatorname{Var}(\tilde\phi)$ with the Hessian of the conditional cross-entropy. (The
parenthesized labels $(A)$ and $(C)$ follow the companion derivation and are distinct
from Term $A$ and Term $B$ of @eq-split: both pieces live inside Term $A$.) The
gradient $\alpha$ is *not* zero — a tempting appeal to Gibbs' inequality fails because
$\alpha$ varies the sampling distribution of the completions, not the evaluation point of
the log-density — and for the multivariate normal it is supported entirely on the
covariance parameters ($\alpha_\mu = 0$, $\alpha_\Sigma \neq 0$). The leading parts of
$(A)$ and $(C)$ cancel exactly when the missing and observed units share a
conditioning-variable distribution, i.e. under MCAR; under MAR their sum is the
design-imbalance term, which for the bivariate monotone normal has the closed form
$$ (A)+(C) \;=\; \frac{n_{\text{mis}}}{n_{\text{obs}}} \Big[\, 1 - \tfrac12 \operatorname{tr}\big(Q_{\text{mis}} Q_{\text{obs}}^{-1}\big) \Big] + O(n^{-1}), $$ {#eq-ac}
with $Q_{\text{mis}}, Q_{\text{obs}}$ the conditioning-variable second-moment matrices of
the missing and observed units — zero exactly when the two distributions agree. For
non-monotone patterns, where the observed-data likelihood does not factor, the
covariance-MLE bias entering $(A)+(C)$ is obtained from the general second-order
(Cox–Snell) bias of the MLE, verified symbolically by two computer-algebra systems and
carried to general $p$.

Three statements about $(A)+(C)$ have different evidential standing, and we keep them
separate. *Firm* (proved, with symbolic verification): its structure, its sign, its
$O(1)$ order under MAR, its exact vanishing under MCAR, the closed form @eq-ac, and the
information convention — the realized (observed) information of
@kenwardLikelihoodBasedFrequentist1998, whose gap from the naive expected information is
itself derived in closed form. *Measured but imprecise*: its absolute magnitude. The
per-replicate statistic is heavy-tailed with standard deviation growing as $\sqrt n$, so
direct Monte Carlo estimates scatter without trend around the analytic leading-order
value ($\approx -0.22$ in the four-variate design); a variance-reduced
estimator shows the correction beyond that value is $O(1/n)$, but conditionally on the
analytic anchor (@sec-discussion, G1). *Structural*: comparisons involve $(A)+(C)$ only through
differences across candidates fit to the same imputations, which cancel the common
heavy-tailed noise; the comparison-relevant differential is derived and measured
precisely in @sec-lrt.

Summing the two terms gives @eq-thm. $\square$

## Scope, and a consistency check

The headline constant requires an estimated scale. With a known scale and a location-only
fit the conditional missing-data entropy is parameter-free, the entropy gradient
vanishes, and a fourth-moment calculation shows the curvature piece survives
uncancelled at exactly $-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ — even under MCAR. The
net bias then collapses to
$$ \mathbb{E}[T]_{\text{known scale}} \;=\; \begin{cases} 0 & \text{deterministic FIML}, \\[2pt] -\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}) & \text{proper MI}, \end{cases} $$ {#eq-knownscale}
the two arms differing by the posterior-draw imputation variance
$C_n = \tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ — so deterministic and proper imputation
are *not* interchangeable for a known-scale fit. A dedicated experiment with
$R = 2\times10^5$ replications confirms all four values to within 4% (@sec-numerics).
Every model compared in the applications below estimates a covariance, so the
applications sit squarely in the estimated-scale regime.

The same total has a more compact reading that serves as a consistency check. Writing
$C_n(\theta)$ for the (negative) conditional entropy of the missing block given the
observed block — for the normal, a function of $\Sigma$ alone — the bias @eq-thm equals
the *plug-in bias* $\Delta_n = \mathbb{E}[C_n(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}) - C_n(\theta_0)]$
of evaluating that entropy at the estimate rather than the truth. The bridge is the
single curvature identity $\nabla^2 C_n(\theta_0) = H_{\phi\phi} +
I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}$, which regroups the $(A)$/main/$(C)$ split of the proof
into one expansion and also delivers the known-scale collapse as the special case where
$C_n$ is parameter-free. Because the normal density carries no parameter-free,
$Y_{\text{mis}}$-dependent additive constant, $T$ — and with it $\Delta_n$ — does not
depend on any normalization convention. Both readings are verified symbolically and by
direct Monte Carlo.

The pre-registered Monte Carlo confirmation of @eq-thm is reported in @sec-numerics; its
headline figure is the inverse-variance-pooled estimate
$\hat T = 2.43 \pm 0.26$ against the $(A)+(C)$-augmented target range $2.42$–$2.55$ in
the four-variate MAR design.

# Application I: likelihood-ratio comparison {#sec-lrt}

@thm-bias prices the bias of one model's $\bar Q_\infty$. A likelihood-ratio comparison
involves two such quantities at once — the same imputed data, two nested fits — and
everything in this section follows from asking what survives the subtraction.

Let the null model be a smooth submodel $\theta = g(\gamma)$ with Jacobian $G$ (full
column rank) and $q_{\mathrm{d}} = k - k_0$ tested constraints. Both models are fit to the
*same* imputations: the MI likelihood-ratio numerator is
$$ \hat d_L \;=\; 2\big[\bar Q_\infty(\hat\psi^{*}) - \bar Q_\infty(\hat\psi_0^{*})\big], \qquad \hat\psi^{*} = \arg\max_\theta \bar Q_\infty(\theta), \quad \hat\psi_0^{*} = \arg\max_{\theta = g(\gamma)} \bar Q_\infty(\theta), $$ {#eq-dl}
the infinite-$m$ limit of the maximize-the-average statistic @eq-bg-dl of
@chanMultipleImprovementsMultiple2022. Note that $\hat\psi_0^{*}$ maximizes the *shared*
$\bar Q_\infty$ over the null manifold — it is not the null model's own observed-data
MLE. The complete-data counterpart $\hat d_{\text{com}}$ has null expectation
$q_{\mathrm{d}} + O(n^{-1})$ by Wilks' theorem, and the object of interest is the
differential $\hat d_L - \hat d_{\text{com}}$.

::: {#prp-lrt}
## Differential bias at the null

Under R1–R7, at the null,
$$ \mathbb{E}\big[\hat d_L - \hat d_{\text{com}}\big] \;=\; \operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}_\perp) + O(n^{-1}), $$ {#eq-lrt-bias}
where
$\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}_\perp) := \operatorname{tr}(I_{\text{obs}}^{-1} I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}})
- \operatorname{tr}\big[(G^\top I_{\text{com}} G)^{-1} G^\top I_{\text{com}} I_{\text{obs}}^{-1} I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}} G\big]$
is the missing information of the tested directions, projected onto the null tangent
space **in the $I_{\text{com}}$ metric**.
:::

The metric is the substance of the result. The score of $\bar Q_\infty$ at the truth has
variance built from $I_{\text{com}} I_{\text{obs}}^{-1} I_{\text{com}}$, while its
curvature converges to $I_{\text{obs}} + I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}} = I_{\text{com}}$ —
the EM identity that the Q-function's curvature is the *complete-data* information
(@sec-background). The two are consistent only together: the unconstrained maximizer of
$\bar Q_\infty$ must reproduce $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$ (EM self-consistency), and it
does so only with curvature $I_{\text{com}}$. The constrained fit therefore projects in
the $I_{\text{com}}$ metric, and taking expectations of the resulting difference of
quadratic forms gives @eq-lrt-bias. Substituting $I_{\text{obs}}$ for the curvature —
equivalently, conflating $\hat\psi_0^{*}$ with the null model's own observed-data MLE —
is the natural error here, and it reproduces exactly the naive formula that the next
proposition shows always overstates. The derivation was carried through three independent
routes with the projection algebra verified symbolically; on a four-variate design built
to separate the two candidate formulas ($H_0\!:\mu_1 = 0$ with $X_1$ missing for most
units under MAR), @eq-lrt-bias gives $2.64$ where the naive difference gives $8.47$, and
the Monte Carlo mean lands on @eq-lrt-bias to within sampling error — $52$ standard
errors from the naive value (@sec-numerics).

::: {#prp-naive}
## The naive difference always overstates

With $\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}_0)$ the null model's own self-contained trace
correction, always
$$ \operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}_\perp) \;\le\; \operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}) - \operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}_0), $$ {#eq-overstate}
with equality if and only if $\operatorname{col}(I_{\text{obs}}^{1/2} G)$ is an invariant
subspace of the standardized missing information
$H = I_{\text{obs}}^{-1/2} I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}} I_{\text{obs}}^{-1/2}$ — in
particular under equal fractions of missing information, where both sides equal
$r\,q_{\mathrm{d}}$.
:::

The proof is one matrix inequality (a Gram-matrix Schur complement), and the gap has an
exact closed form: partitioning $H$ by retained directions (block $H_{11}$) against
tested directions (coupling $H_{12}$),
$$ \big[\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}) - \operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}_0)\big] - \operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}_\perp) \;=\; \operatorname{tr}\big[(I + H_{11})^{-1} H_{12} H_{12}^\top\big] \;\ge\; 0, $$ {#eq-gap}
zero exactly when $H_{12} = 0$ — when tested and retained directions carry independent
missing information. Correcting an MI deviance comparison by the difference of the two
models' self-contained traces therefore *over*-corrects at the null whenever the tested
directions mix unequally-missing information: by about $5\%$ in the pre-registered
covariance-test design (which sits near the invariance case), by a factor above three in
the $\mu_1$ design above.

::: {#prp-pairing}
## Pairing collapses the noise

Let $D = (\hat d_L - \hat d_{\text{com}})/2$ be the per-dataset paired differential. At
the null and under local alternatives $\theta_n = g(\gamma_0) + h/\sqrt n$,
$\operatorname{sd}(D) = O(1)$, against $O(\sqrt n)$ for either level separately. At a
fixed alternative the cancellation fails and $\operatorname{sd}(D)$ reverts to
$O(\sqrt n)$.
:::

The mechanism is exact cancellation of realizations, not averaging: the $O_p(\sqrt n)$
noise of each level lives in fit-independent realized constants — the entropy gradient
contracted with the shared imputation error, plus the realized
$\ell_{\text{com}} - \ell_{\text{obs}}$ at the truth — and both fits maximize the *same*
realized $\bar Q_\infty$ built from the *same* imputation parameter, so these pieces are
literally identical in the two levels and cancel dataset by dataset. Empirically, at the
null, $\operatorname{sd}$ of the single-model statistic grows
$18.4 \to 26.6 \to 36.5$ across $N = 500 \to 1000 \to 2000$ while
$\operatorname{sd}(D)$ stays flat at $1.58 \to 1.47 \to 1.57$; at the fixed alternative
the $\sqrt n$ growth returns ($12.4 \to 16.8 \to 24.9$; @sec-numerics). This is why every
quantitative claim about the comparison-relevant correction in this paper is estimated by
paired contrasts, never by differencing separately estimated levels.

::: {#prp-ac-diff}
## The design-imbalance differential cancels at the null

The $(A)+(C)$ contributions of the two levels are built from the entropy gradient, the
cross-entropy curvature, and the shared imputation error — properties of the
*imputation*, not of which model is fit. At the null they are identical realizations and
cancel exactly to leading order; under local alternatives the differential is
$O(n^{-1/2})$; it decouples into a genuine $O(1)$ differential only when the candidates'
pseudo-true values are separated at $O(1)$ — the regime of
@vuongLikelihoodRatioTests1989. Separation, not nesting, is the criterion: a nested but
*false* restriction triggers the decoupling just as a non-nested pair does.
:::

A direct measurement confirms the mechanism and adds one quantitative refinement. Fitting
compound-symmetry and AR(1) covariance candidates to the same congenial imputations under
a strong MAR design — pseudo-true correlations well separated — and isolating the
design-imbalance contribution by differencing against an MCAR twin ($20{,}000$ replicates
per cell): the per-candidate $(A)+(C)$ *level* is a large $O(1)$, about $+2.5$ deviance
units, that collapses to zero under MCAR; the two candidates' levels decouple ($+2.33$ vs
$+2.85$); and the paired noise returns to $O(\sqrt n)$. But the *differential* between
these two similar candidates is below Monte Carlo resolution — they respond to the
imbalance so similarly that their contributions nearly cancel. A maximally dissimilar
pair — a structure-blind diagonal model against AR(1) — carries a resolved differential
of $-1.2$, $3.5$ standard errors from zero, three to four times the similar pair's bound: the
differential's magnitude scales with how differently the competitors handle the missing
data. One caveat travels with the dissimilar case and is restated in @sec-discussion: a
badly misspecified candidate also carries a mechanism-independent misspecification
$O(1)$, separable from the design-imbalance term by the MCAR contrast but capable of
partially offsetting it in the net ranking bias.

## Which procedures inherit the bias

At the null, @eq-lrt-bias is precisely the mean inflation that a correctly calibrated
reference distribution already absorbs: the limiting law of $\hat d_L$ is the weighted
sum $\sum_{j=1}^{q_{\mathrm{d}}} \lambda_j \chi^2_1$ with weights
$\lambda_j = 1 + r_{\perp,j}$, where $r_{\perp,j}$ are the eigenvalues of the projected
missing-information matrix behind $\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}_\perp)$
[@chanGeneralFeasibleTests2022, eq. 1.10], and
$$ \sum_{j=1}^{q_{\mathrm{d}}} \lambda_j \;=\; q_{\mathrm{d}} + \operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}_\perp). $$ {#eq-spectrum-sum}
A test that refers the *uncorrected* numerator to such a reference is therefore
approximately calibrated at the null, and additionally correcting the numerator
double-counts — consistent with the pre-registered null rates ($0.042$ uncorrected
against Chan's simulated reference; $0.034$, slightly conservative, with the additional
correction; @sec-numerics). The differential bias matters instead exactly where no
reference distribution stands between the statistic and its use:
information-criterion ranking, which compares penalized $-2\bar Q_\infty$ values
directly; explicit numerator corrections, which must use @eq-lrt-bias rather than the
naive difference (@prp-naive); and non-nested comparison, where the design-imbalance
differential and the heavy-tailed noise both return (@prp-ac-diff). The first of these is
taken up next.

**Scope.** The propositions above are proved for the deterministic-FIML $\bar Q_\infty$
(imputation at the point $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$). Under proper MI the imputation-side
quantities acquire posterior smearing but remain common to both fits — the same
posterior, the same draws — so the cancellation structure of @prp-pairing and
@prp-ac-diff and the leading-order form of @eq-lrt-bias carry over structurally; this
carry-over is argued, not separately measured, and is listed as a limitation in
@sec-discussion (G2). A blind re-derivation by an independent model lineage, given only the
setup, reproduced every load-bearing conclusion — the $I_{\text{com}}$ metric,
@eq-lrt-bias term by term, the definite sign of @eq-overstate with a third independent
proof, and the noise orders and exact null cancellation — and an adversarial pass
instructed to break each claim sustained all four.

# Application II: information-criterion model selection {#sec-ic}

The likelihood-ratio application referred a statistic to a reference distribution. Model
selection by information criterion has no reference distribution: it compares penalized
deviances as numbers, across candidates, and takes the smallest. Whatever bias each
candidate's $-2\bar Q_\infty$ carries lands directly in the ranking.

## The corrected criterion

The MI criterion built on the averaged deviance is
$\mathrm{AIC}_{\mathrm{MI}}(k) = -2\bar Q_\infty(\hat\psi^{*}_k) + 2q_k$, with $q_k$ the
candidate's parameter count. By @thm-bias each candidate's $\bar Q_\infty$ overstates its
complete-data target by $\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}_k)$ — and the bias is
*model-specific*, because $\mathrm{RIV}_k$ is computed on candidate $k$'s own parameter
space. The corrected criterion is
$$ \mathrm{AIC}_{\mathrm{MI}}^{c}(k) \;=\; -2\bar Q_\infty(\hat\psi^{*}_k) + 2q_k + \operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}_k). $$ {#eq-ic-corrected}
As a penalty this reproduces $\mathrm{AIC}_{x;y}$ of
@shimodairaInformationCriterionModel2017, whose missing-data surcharge
$\operatorname{tr}(I_{z\mid y} I_y^{-1})$ is exactly half that of AICcd
[@cavanaughAkaikeInformationCriterion1998a] and PDIO; what @thm-bias adds is the anatomy
(imputation bias against estimation mismatch), the MAR design-imbalance term, and the
proper-MI scope (@sec-intro). The ranking consequence is immediate: for two candidates
the uncorrected criterion difference carries a differential bias
$-[\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}_j) - \operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}_k)]$, so the
candidate with more missing information about its own parameters looks artificially
better by exactly its surplus of missing information.

Two qualifications follow from @sec-lrt. First, candidate sets are generally not nested
chains, and for candidate pairs with $O(1)$-separated pseudo-true values the
design-imbalance differential of @prp-ac-diff is a genuine $O(1)$ that
@eq-ic-corrected does not remove and no reference absorbs — small for similar candidates,
growing with dissimilarity. Second, the per-model
$\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}_k)$ corrections are exactly the self-contained levels
whose difference @prp-naive shows can overstate the projected complement trace; the
candidate family studied below happens to sit at the exact equality case, a structural
fact established at the end of this section.

## Pre-registered selection evidence

The pre-registered study draws four-variate normal data whose only nonzero correlations
are $\sigma_{13} = \sigma_{24} = \rho$, imposes 40% MAR missingness, and ranks four
zero-pattern covariance candidates — diagonal, wrong off-diagonals, the truth, and
saturated — by MI-AIC under congenial imputation. At $N = 500$ with $\rho = 0.40$,
uncorrected MI-AIC selects the true model in $0.674$ of replicates against an oracle
(complete-data AIC) rate of $0.898$ — and **100% of its misclassifications land on the
saturated, largest-RIV candidate**, the directional signature of the model-specific
bias. The corrected criterion recovers selection to $0.816$: substantial, but visibly
short of the oracle (@sec-numerics). That residual is not an error in the trace; it is
the subject of the rest of this section.

## Beyond the mean: distribution-level calibration

Selection depends on more than a mean. At the null the limiting law of each anchored
deviance is $\sum_j \lambda_j \chi^2_1$ with every weight $\lambda_j = 1 + r_{\perp,j}
\ge 1$ (@eq-spectrum-sum), so even a correctly centered criterion has inflated spread —
and inflated spread flips rankings. This motivates a graded contrast, pre-registered in
full before any code was written: match the first moment, the first two moments, or the
entire null distribution, and measure what each step achieves.

*The working statistic.* Anchor every candidate at the saturated model fit to the same
imputations, $T_k = 2[\bar Q_\infty(\hat\psi^{*}_{\text{sat}}) -
\bar Q_\infty(\hat\psi^{*}_k)] \ge 0$. Anchoring costs nothing — AIC ranking is invariant
to the common shift — and has three consequences: the anchor is the congenial imputation
model itself, so the heavy-tailed realized $(A)+(C)$ component and the $O_p(\sqrt n)$
noise cancel dataset by dataset (the @prp-pairing mechanism applied to every candidate);
every $T_k$ is a likelihood-ratio statistic with an analytic per-model null law — the
weighted-$\chi^2$ spectrum of @eq-spectrum-sum, available without knowing the global
truth; and for
block-diagonal zero patterns the constrained maximizer is closed-form, giving
$T_k = N[\log|\hat\Sigma_k| - \log|S^{*}|]$ exactly.

*The three maps.* Let $\lambda_{k,1}, \dots, \lambda_{k,q_{d,k}}$ be the analytic null
weights of candidate $k$, with $\sum_j \lambda_{k,j} = q_{d,k} +
\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}_{\perp,k})$ and every $\lambda_{k,j} \ge 1$:

1. *Mean:* $T^{(1)}_k = T_k - \operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}_{\perp,k})$. A linear
   per-model shift; it telescopes, so all pairwise comparisons are simultaneously
   mean-corrected.
2. *Two moments:* $T^{(2)}_k = a_k T_k + b_k$ with
   $a_k = \sqrt{q_{d,k} / \sum_j \lambda_{k,j}^2}$ and
   $b_k = q_{d,k} - a_k \sum_j \lambda_{k,j}$ — the unique affine map carrying the null
   law's mean and variance onto $\chi^2_{q_{d,k}}$'s. Matching a misbehaving statistic's
   first two moments is the standard repair in the structural-equation tradition — the
   scaled difference statistic of @satorraEnsuringPositivenessScaled2010 matches the
   mean, and the mean-and-variance-adjusted statistics of @asparouhovRobustChiSquare2006
   match both from the same inputs $\operatorname{tr}(M)$, $\operatorname{tr}(M^2)$ —
   but here the coefficients are derived from the analytic null law rather than
   estimated from sandwich matrices.
3. *All moments:* $T^{(3)}_k = F^{-1}_{\chi^2_{q_{d,k}}}\big(F_{W_k}(T_k)\big)$ with
   $W_k = \sum_j \lambda_{k,j} \chi^2_1$ — the equipercentile equating function
   $e_Y(x) = G^{-1}[F(x)]$ of observed-score test equating
   [@kolenTestEquatingScaling2014], applied with the analytic null law as $F$ and the
   complete-data $\chi^2$ as $G$; by the probability integral transform it matches the
   entire null distribution. The weighted-$\chi^2$ distribution function is evaluated by
   numerical inversion of the characteristic function
   [@imhofComputingDistributionQuadratic1961; @daviesAlgorithm155Distribution1980]; a
   simulated reference in the style of Chan's Monte Carlo null was the pre-registered
   fallback and was never needed (0 of 18,000 evaluations).

The pre-registration, written before the script existed, predicted the split that the
data then delivered: each step closes more of the null-side calibration gap, while on
the noncentral side the two stronger maps actively *shrink* evidence against
misspecified candidates — the affine map by $a_k < 1$, the equating map by
$\approx 1/\lambda_{\max}$ in the far tail — because no transform built from the
observed data can restore destroyed Fisher information.

## What calibration achieves, and what it cannot

Three cells, $N = 500$, $R = 2000$ each, 40% non-monotone MAR: the pre-registered design
($\rho = 0.40$), a weak-signal stress cell ($\rho = 0.10$, near-tied candidates), and a
junk cell ($\rho = 0$, truth diagonal, every anchored statistic null-type). This is a
separate pre-registered run from the 60-cell sweep above, so its $\rho = 0.40$ rates
differ from the sweep's by Monte Carlo error ($0.899/0.678/0.814$ here against
$0.898/0.674/0.816$ there). True-model
selection rates (Monte Carlo se $\approx 0.010$):

| Cell | oracle | uncorrected | mean | two-moment | equating |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\rho = 0.40$ | 0.899 | 0.678 | 0.814 | 0.904 | 0.903 |
| $\rho = 0.10$ | 0.820 | 0.582 | 0.650 | 0.648 | 0.650 |
| $\rho = 0$ (junk) | 0.727 | 0.422 | 0.585 | 0.702 | 0.696 |

Every pre-registered pass criterion was met. Four findings summarize the results.

*The null side calibrates completely.* On the true model's anchored statistic the
Kolmogorov–Smirnov distance to the paired complete-data statistic falls
$0.281 \to 0.228 \to 0.026 \to 0.019$ across the four arms, and the variance ratio falls
from $3.18$ (uncorrected *and* mean-corrected) to $0.97$. The mean correction's paired
gap, $0.076 \pm 0.100$, is itself a direct Monte Carlo confirmation of @eq-lrt-bias at
$q_{\mathrm{d}} = 4$. In the junk cell, where all anchored statistics are null-type, equating
calibrates all of them ($D_{KS} \le 0.035$).

*Where overfit flips drive the errors, distribution matching reaches the oracle.* In the
$\rho = 0.40$ design the mean-corrected residual ($0.814$ vs $0.899$) is variance, not
mean: the two-moment and equating arms select at $0.904$ and $0.903$, statistically
indistinguishable from complete-data AIC. The junk cell shows the same mechanism at
larger scale, closing 90% of the uncorrected-to-oracle gap.

*The noncentral side shrinks as the law predicts.* The underfit candidate's
mean statistic, relative to its complete-data counterpart: $1.002$ under the mean
correction (evidence preserved), $0.586$ under two moments (predicted $a_k = 0.570$),
$0.362$ under equating (predicted far-tail factor $1/\lambda_{\max} = 0.398$).
Conservatism against misspecified candidates is the cost of null-side calibration, and
the factor is known in advance.

*Where selection is genuinely hard, nothing closes the floor.* In the weak-signal cell
the oracle itself drops to $0.820$ and no correction passes $0.650$. The $0.17$ residual
is information loss, not calibration error: the stronger arms' null-side gains are fully
offset by underfit retention (they keep too-small models at rate $0.270$ against the
oracle's $0.091$). This floor is a measurement on these designs, not a theorem
(@sec-discussion, G4).

Two structural facts limit how far these results generalize, and both are exact. First,
in this candidate family the naive trace difference and the exact complement trace agree
to machine precision ($3.6 \times 10^{-15}$) — not luck: block-diagonal covariance
patterns decompose both informations over within-block and cross-block coordinates,
which is precisely the equality condition of @prp-naive. The overstatement is therefore
structurally invisible in this family and material only for constraints that do not
block-decompose — in the $\mu_1$ design of @sec-lrt it is a factor above three. (An
earlier sweep arm that applied a moment map built from naive-difference inputs scores
$0.824$ in the $\rho = 0.40$ cell against the derived two-moment arm's $0.904$.) Second,
per-model marginal transforms cannot calibrate a *difference* distribution: the
dependence between two candidates' scores is invariant under marginal maps, and in the
weak-signal near-tied pair the equated score difference has mean $6.10$ and sd $5.99$
against the complete-data $\Delta$AIC's $10.00$ and $7.10$. Selection-aware refinements —
de-shrinkage of estimated noncentrality, joint calibration of score vectors — are
developed separately.

These limits differ in standing, and we state which is which: the noncentral shrinkage
and the difference-distribution invariance are proved and exactly confirmed; the
weak-signal floor is a measured residual. As in @sec-lrt, everything here is stated and
measured for the deterministic-FIML $\bar Q_\infty$; the anchoring cancellations are
properties of sharing the imputation model, so the construction carries to proper MI
unchanged, and the pre-registered sweep's proper-MI cells track the FIML cells within
$0.011$ on the headline rates (@sec-numerics).

# Numerical studies {#sec-numerics}

Every quantitative claim above traces to a study whose predictions and pass criteria were
frozen before execution. The pre-registration documents, their amendment histories, the
seeds, and the per-study caches are committed in the repository (`todo/004`, `todo/027`,
`todo/029`; `verification/`); the companion derivation carries the full grading. This
section describes the shared apparatus once and then states what each study found,
including the predictions that were *not* met.

**Apparatus.** All designs are multivariate normal (bivariate and four-variate), with
monotone and non-monotone missingness patterns and MCAR, MAR, and (for robustness arms)
MNAR mechanisms; missingness rates near 40% in the main cells. Two imputation engines
are used. The deterministic arm evaluates $\bar Q_\infty$ analytically at the FIML
estimate $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$ — the EM Q-function itself, with no simulation error.
The proper-MI arm draws imputations by expectation–maximization with bootstrapping (EMB),
as implemented in Amelia [@honakerAmeliaIIProgram2011]: EM run on bootstrap resamples
yields approximate posterior draws of $(\mu, \Sigma)$, from which completions are drawn.
The exact alternative — data augmentation, whose draws follow the observed-data posterior
at any sample size — was not run; the resulting scope limit is stated below and in
@sec-discussion.

**Theorem validation.** The pre-registered bias-decomposition study estimates
$\mathbb{E}[A]$, $\mathbb{E}[B]$, $\mathbb{E}[T]$ across patterns, mechanisms, and sample
sizes against the analytic $\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$. One caveat is part
of the record: the study was pre-registered against the half-trace *alone*, before the
$O(1)$ MAR term had been derived, and the per-replicate statistic is heavy-tailed with
standard deviation growing as $\sqrt n$ — so at fixed replication the larger-$n$ cells
cannot resolve an $O(1)$ offset, and one cell ($N = 1000$) misses the original target at
$2.6$ standard errors. Pooling the four sample sizes by inverse variance gives
$\hat T = 2.43 \pm 0.26$ against the pre-registered target $2.77$ and the
$(A)+(C)$-augmented range $2.42$–$2.55$: the pooled pre-registered data corroborate the
completed theorem, under a criterion amendment documented in the pre-registration's
version history. At the component level the individual terms miss their targets by
roughly $9$–$20\%$ at the sample sizes studied — $\mathbb{E}[B]$ overshoots its
$-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ target by $-0.3$ to $-0.55$ with tight Monte
Carlo error — which is why every quantitative claim in this paper is stated for totals
and paired differentials, never for components. Two targeted experiments complete the
picture: imputing from the truth instead of the fitted model reverses the sign of the
total exactly as the fitted-versus-oracle distinction of @sec-setup requires, and the
known-scale experiment ($R = 2 \times 10^5$) reproduces all four values of
@eq-knownscale within 4%.

**Likelihood-ratio studies.** The pre-registered rejection study confirms the absorption
property of @sec-lrt: the uncorrected numerator referred to Chan's simulated reference
rejects at $0.042$ at the null, and additionally correcting the numerator gives $0.034$ —
slightly conservative, as double-counting predicts. (The study's own committed correction
arm used the naive trace difference as input; in its near-invariance design the
difference from @eq-lrt-bias is about 5% and immaterial, but the general form is
@eq-lrt-bias.) The same study's power arms, with a post-hoc size-adjusted analysis
(`verification/W2-lrt-power.R`, `verification/W2-size-adjusted-power.R`), are the
partial empirical support cited for the power conjecture of @sec-discussion. The discrimination experiment for @prp-lrt ran $R = 2000$ replicates on
the $\mu_1$ design where the two formulas separate widely: observed differentials
$2.639 \pm 0.113$ ($N = 500$) and $2.701 \pm 0.112$ ($N = 1000$) against the predicted
$2.64$ and the naive $8.47$ — $52$ standard errors from the naive value. The pairing
study behind @prp-pairing measured $\operatorname{sd}(T) = 18.4 \to 26.6 \to 36.5$
against $\operatorname{sd}(D) = 1.58 \to 1.47 \to 1.57$ across
$N = 500 \to 1000 \to 2000$ at the null, with the $\sqrt n$ growth returning at the
fixed alternative ($12.4 \to 16.8 \to 24.9$).

**Model selection.** The committed 60-cell selection sweep produced the headline rates of
@sec-ic (at $N = 500$, deterministic FIML: oracle $0.898$, uncorrected $0.674$, corrected
$0.816$), with 100% of uncorrected misclassifications on the saturated candidate in every
congenial cell. The distribution-matching contrast ran three cells at $R = 2000$ under
its own pre-registration; its results table appears in @sec-ic, every pass criterion was
met, and the internal validity checks held on every replicate: the spectrum-trace
identity to $3 \times 10^{-15}$, all null-law weights $\ge 1$, and zero failures of the
characteristic-function inversion (the pre-registered simulated-reference fallback was
never invoked, in 18,000 evaluations).

**Non-nested measurement.** A pre-registered measurement ($N \in \{500, 1000, 2000\}$,
$20{,}000$ replicates per cell, with an MCAR twin isolating the mechanism) targeted the
one derived-but-unmeasured quantity in @sec-lrt: the design-imbalance differential of
@prp-ac-diff for genuinely separated pairs. The mechanism was confirmed exactly — each
candidate's $(A)+(C)$ level is a large $O(1)$ ($\approx +2.5$ deviance units, tens of
standard errors from zero) that collapses to zero under MCAR; the two candidates' levels
decouple ($+2.33$ vs $+2.85$); the paired noise returns to $O(\sqrt n)$; and a
nested-but-false restriction triggers the same return, confirming that separation rather
than nesting is the criterion. The pre-registered *headline* for the similar pair
(compound symmetry vs AR(1)) was **not** met: its differential sits below Monte Carlo
resolution (a pilot's apparent $-2$ dissolved in the full run), and we report that
failure as the discipline requires. The pre-registered dissimilar-pair follow-up
(structure-blind diagonal vs AR(1), committed before its code) resolved the predicted
scaling: an isolated design-imbalance differential of $-1.2$, $3.5$ standard errors from
zero, three to four times the similar pair's bound and stable across $N$. A byproduct supplied
the first measured case off the equality condition of @prp-naive: for these curved
candidates the naive trace difference overstates the exact complement trace by
$\approx 9\%$ (`verification/cache/w4-nonnested-summary.csv`).

**Engine sensitivity.** On the most demanding cells — non-monotone MAR with $N$ as small
as $200$, where the bootstrap approximation to the imputation posterior is weakest — the
sample RIV built from the EMB draws reproduces the observed-data RIV within $2$–$3\%$
with no widening as $N$ grows, and model selection agrees between deterministic FIML and
the EMB analysis within $0.011$ on the recovery gap. This robustness is empirical and
scoped to the designs studied: the EMB sampler is only approximately Bayesian, data
augmentation was not run, and a fully Bayesian sampler remains the conservative choice
when the fraction of missing information is large (@sec-discussion, G3).

# Discussion {#sec-discussion}

The result is scoped by its own mechanism. The bias is present only when the fit
estimates a scale or covariance, so that the conditional missing-data entropy depends on
the estimated parameters; a known-scale, location-only fit has no such bias, which is why
the estimated-scale clause is in the theorem rather than a footnote. Congeniality is
assumed throughout — what the bias becomes without it
[@mengMultipleImputationInferencesUncongenial1994a] is a separate question we do not take
up. Beyond these scope conditions, six specific limits remain, each stated here next to
the claim it qualifies.

**G1 — the absolute magnitude of $(A)+(C)$ is not independently pinned** (qualifies
@thm-bias). Firm: its structure, sign, $O(1)$ order, exact MCAR vanishing, the bivariate
closed form @eq-ac, and the $O(1/n)$ order of the correction beyond the leading-order
analytic value. Not firm: the absolute magnitude itself — the direct Monte Carlo
estimates are heavy-tailed and untrended, and the variance-reduced estimate of the
higher-order remainder is conditional on the analytic anchor it is paired against. Every
comparison in this paper therefore uses $(A)+(C)$ only through the better-conditioned
paired differential.

**G2 — the proper-MI carry-over of the likelihood-ratio propositions is structural, not
separately measured** (qualifies @sec-lrt). The propositions are proved and
Monte-Carlo-confirmed for the deterministic-FIML $\bar Q_\infty$; under proper MI the
imputation-side quantities remain common to both fits, which preserves the cancellation
arguments, but no dedicated proper-MI replication of the differential experiments exists.

**G3 — imputation-engine robustness is empirical and scoped** (qualifies
@sec-numerics). EMB tracks deterministic FIML within the stated tolerances on the designs
studied; exact data augmentation was not run, and nothing here establishes
engine-independence beyond those designs.

**G4 — the weak-signal floor is a measurement, not an impossibility proof** (qualifies
@sec-ic). The $0.17$ residual in the $\rho = 0.10$ cell is information loss as measured
through these corrections; no argument here shows that *no* estimator built from the same
observed data could do better.

**G5 — for dissimilar candidate pairs, a mechanism-independent misspecification $O(1)$
coexists with the design-imbalance $O(1)$** (qualifies @prp-ac-diff and its use in
@sec-ic). The MCAR contrast separates the two in measurement, but they can reinforce or
partially offset in the net ranking bias a criterion sees; we make no claim that the
design-imbalance term dominates every pair.

**G6 — all instantiation is multivariate normal** (qualifies @thm-bias). The theorem is
stated for general regular likelihoods with an estimated scale, but every constant is
verified — symbolically and by simulation — only in the normal family.

One question is left as a conjecture: whether the bias-corrected likelihood-ratio
comparison dominates its uncorrected counterpart in power uniformly. The pre-registered
likelihood-ratio study's power arms (@sec-numerics) provide partial support; we state no
theorem. Three directions seem most
worth pursuing. First, the connection to covariate shift: the concluding section of
@shimodairaInformationCriterionModel2017 names the combination of a missing mechanism
with other sampling mechanisms as future work, and the design-imbalance term derived
here — nonzero exactly when the missing and observed units differ on the conditioning
variables — is a step into that program, whose weighting machinery exists
[@shimodairahidetoshiImprovingPredictiveInference2000]. Second, an exact-engine
replication (data augmentation) of the headline studies would convert G3 from an
empirical tolerance into a tested equivalence. Third, the distribution-level calibration
program beyond per-model null maps — de-shrinkage of estimated noncentrality, joint
calibration of score vectors across candidates — is developed separately, as is the
extension beyond the normal family.

# Provenance and authorship statement {#sec-provenance}

This is an AI-assisted derivation with a human prompter of record (Marcus Waldman,
ORCID-verified), prepared for the Journal for AI Generated Papers under its transparency
norms. The provenance record is part of the submission: the companion sourced derivation
(`manuscript/derivation.qmd`) pins every external claim to a source passage (reproduced
verbatim for review) in a locally archived copy and every number to a committed artifact;
the `verification/` directory and the `todo/` pre-registration documents contain the
pre-registered studies, their frozen originals, amendment histories, and caches; the
load-bearing analytic claims were additionally cross-checked by an independent
model lineage under a blind adversarial protocol, with the grading records committed.
Citation discipline is enforced mechanically: no reference appears in this manuscript
without a locally archived, session-read copy of the source.

# References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
